title: On the craze for eating God's way
gist: The viral 'biblical eating' trend, monetised by trad-wife influencers with hundreds of thousands of followers, is a recurring American Christian food culture dating to the 1830s; it overlaps with the wellness/Make America Healthy Again movement and a commercial 'raw' food and supplement ecosystem.

insights.claim: Biblical-eating influencers command large monetised audiences (e.g. Janice Armstrong ~598k Instagram followers, Kayla Bundy ~500k TikTok), banking six or seven figures from brand partnerships.
insights.speaker: Zoe Strimpel
insights.so_what: Quantifies a sizeable creator-economy niche fusing faith, wellness and commerce.
insights.quote: even while banking six or seven figures from lucrative brand partnerships

insights.claim: The trend overlaps with the Make America Healthy Again movement's hostility to pasteurisation, promoting raw milk, bone broth, sardines and local-only ingredients.
insights.speaker: Zoe Strimpel
insights.so_what: Maps the consumer overlap between religious-diet content and the broader anti-processed-food wellness market.
insights.quote: the Make America Healthy Again movement hates pasteurisation
insights.locator: Her interpretation of biblical eating is 'raw'

insights.claim: Biblical eating is not new: it traces to Sylvester Graham's 1830s Graham cracker and Kellogg's lust-fighting cornflake, with 1990s revivals like the Hallelujah Diet and 2004's The Maker's Diet.
insights.speaker: Zoe Strimpel
insights.so_what: Frames the trend as a cyclical American phenomenon rather than a one-off fad, suggesting durability.
insights.quote: it is not new - as virtually nothing in the diet world is
insights.locator: The biblical eating trend has been glossed up

insights.claim: The unchanging nature of scripture means the genre's content and aesthetics have barely evolved in 40 years, looking dated even when on-trend.
insights.speaker: Zoe Strimpel
insights.so_what: A product category whose source material caps innovation, a structural ceiling on differentiation.
insights.quote: led to relatively little change in how Christians present the material

insights.claim: Biblical eating has genuine value because, like other current trends, it advocates whole, fibrous, protein-rich foods and avoids ultra-processed fare, though the raw-milk emphasis may be ill-advised.
insights.speaker: Zoe Strimpel
insights.so_what: Separates the sound nutritional core from the riskier 'raw' practices within the trend.
insights.quote: it advocates whole, fibrous, protein-rich foods, and steers away from ultra-processed fare
insights.locator: Is there any value in biblical eating?
